content.text: Archival and Knowledge Transfer on Sunsetting

When a Claude-integrated service is sunset, the accumulated knowledge embedded in its design, prompts, operational patterns, and incident history should not be lost. Engineers who worked on the service may move to other teams; the replacement service or successor product will benefit from understanding what worked, what failed, and why certain design decisions were made. Archival and knowledge transfer converts the service's operational history from tacit knowledge (in the heads of engineers who were there) to documented artefacts that can be consulted by anyone who needs to understand the service's context after it is gone.

attrs.alt: Six archival artefacts produced during a service sunset: system design record, operational runbooks archive, prompt and evaluation library, decision and incident log, replacement service onboarding guide, and archived source code snapshot

content.text: What to Archive and Why

Not everything the service produced or consumed is worth archiving. The goal is to capture the artefacts that are hard to reconstruct from scratch and that have value for future work. Infrastructure configuration that will never be reused, routine deployment logs, and auto-generated build artefacts do not need to be archived. The artefacts that do need archiving fall into six categories.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class ArchivalCategory(str, Enum):
    SYSTEM_DESIGN        = "system_design"     # ADRs, architecture diagrams, API contracts
    PROMPT_LIBRARY       = "prompt_library"    # system prompts, few-shot examples, evals
    OPERATIONAL_RUNBOOKS = "operational_runbooks"  # incident response, deployment guides
    DECISION_LOG         = "decision_log"      # why things were built the way they were
    INCIDENT_HISTORY     = "incident_history"  # post-mortems, root cause analyses
    SOURCE_SNAPSHOT      = "source_snapshot"   # tagged git commit or read-only mirror

class ArchivalUrgency(str, Enum):
    BEFORE_TEARDOWN   = "before_teardown"  # must complete before infra is deleted
    WITHIN_30_DAYS    = "within_30_days"   # knowledge transfer, lower urgency
    ONGOING_AVAILABLE = "ongoing_available"  # source code mirror; always accessible

@dataclass
class ArchivalArtefact:
    category: ArchivalCategory
    name: str
    location: str           # destination: wiki, git repo, S3 archive, etc.
    urgency: ArchivalUrgency
    owner: str
    is_complete: bool = False
    notes: str = ""

    def summary(self) -> str:
        status = "DONE" if self.is_complete else f"PENDING ({self.urgency.value})"
        return f"{self.name} [{self.category.value}] -> {self.location}: {status}"

ARCHIVAL_URGENCY_MAP: dict[ArchivalCategory, ArchivalUrgency] = {
    ArchivalCategory.SYSTEM_DESIGN:        ArchivalUrgency.BEFORE_TEARDOWN,
    ArchivalCategory.PROMPT_LIBRARY:       ArchivalUrgency.BEFORE_TEARDOWN,
    ArchivalCategory.OPERATIONAL_RUNBOOKS: ArchivalUrgency.BEFORE_TEARDOWN,
    ArchivalCategory.DECISION_LOG:         ArchivalUrgency.WITHIN_30_DAYS,
    ArchivalCategory.INCIDENT_HISTORY:     ArchivalUrgency.WITHIN_30_DAYS,
    ArchivalCategory.SOURCE_SNAPSHOT:      ArchivalUrgency.ONGOING_AVAILABLE,
}

The BEFORE_TEARDOWN urgency is the critical constraint: system design records, prompt libraries, and operational runbooks must be archived before the service infrastructure is removed. Once the production database is deleted and the containers are torn down, the only surviving copy of a system prompt that was stored in a configuration service — and not in the code repository — is gone. Infrastructure teardown is a one-way operation; archival gaps discovered after teardown cannot be recovered.
Archiving the Prompt Library and Evaluation Suite

The prompt library is the highest-value artefact for most Claude integrations. System prompts that were iterated over many months to achieve a specific quality level represent significant engineering investment. When the service is sunset, that investment should be transferred to the replacement service or stored for reference rather than lost. The prompt archive must include not just the final production version of each prompt, but the evaluation suite that was used to validate it — because a prompt without its evaluation context cannot be reliably reproduced or adapted.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class PromptVersion:
    version_id: str
    system_prompt: str
    deployed_date: str
    retired_date: str | None
    performance_notes: str   # what changed and why compared to previous version

@dataclass
class EvaluationCase:
    case_id: str
    input_message: str
    expected_output_structure: str  # description, not exact match
    must_contain: list[str]
    must_not_contain: list[str]
    pass_rate_threshold: float      # fraction of runs that must pass

@dataclass
class PromptArchiveEntry:
    feature_name: str
    final_production_version: PromptVersion
    version_history: list[PromptVersion] = field(default_factory=list)
    evaluation_suite: list[EvaluationCase] = field(default_factory=list)
    known_limitations: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    replacement_service_applicable: bool = True

    def archive_document(self) -> str:
        """
        Produces a plain-text archive document suitable for storage
        in a wiki or documentation system.
        """
        lines = [
            f"# Prompt Archive: {self.feature_name}",
            f"## Final Production System Prompt",
            self.final_production_version.system_prompt,
            f"## Evaluation Suite ({len(self.evaluation_suite)} cases)",
        ]
        for case in self.evaluation_suite:
            lines.append(f"### {case.case_id}")
            lines.append(f"Input: {case.input_message}")
            lines.append(f"Must contain: {case.must_contain}")
            lines.append(f"Pass threshold: {case.pass_rate_threshold}")
        lines.append("## Known Limitations")
        lines.extend(f"- {l}" for l in self.known_limitations)
        return "\n".join(lines)

The known_limitations field is the most useful part of the archive for teams building the replacement service. Limitations discovered in production — edge cases where the prompt fails, topic categories where the model produces unexpected output, interaction patterns that trigger inconsistent formatting — are hard-won knowledge. Documenting them in the archive means the replacement service team can write evaluation cases for these known failure modes from day one, rather than rediscovering them after the replacement is deployed.
Knowledge Transfer to the Replacement Service Team

If a replacement service will take over the functionality of the sunset service, a structured knowledge transfer session is more effective than documentation alone. Documentation captures what was built and how; a knowledge transfer session captures why decisions were made, what alternatives were considered and rejected, and what the original team would do differently if starting over. The session format depends on the size of the team and the complexity of the service, but the agenda items are consistent.
Architecture walkthrough:

The original team presents the system design, pointing to the ADRs and explaining the reasoning behind key decisions. The replacement team asks questions about alternatives that were rejected — understanding what was tried and why it didn't work is as valuable as understanding what was chosen.
Prompt evolution history:

Walk through the major iterations of the system prompt, explaining what changed in each version and what prompted the change (a user feedback pattern, a model version upgrade, a new use case). The replacement team learns the reasoning process behind prompt design, not just the final output.
Top incidents post-mortem review:

Review the three to five most significant incidents in the service's history: what happened, what the root cause was, and what mitigations were put in place. Incidents that resulted from model behaviour (unexpected refusals, format drift, hallucinated structured output) are especially relevant for Claude integrations.
Operational pain points:

What aspects of operating the service were harder than expected? What monitoring turned out to be insufficient? What deployment process caused the most incidents? The original team's operational experience is the most actionable input for the replacement service's architecture and operational design.
Open questions:

What problems was the original team actively working on when the sunset decision was made? What known issues were not yet fixed? These represent the knowledge at the leading edge — work that was in progress is often the hardest to document and the most valuable to transfer explicitly.
Source Code Archival and Read-Only Mirror

The service's source code should be archived in a read-only state when the service is decommissioned. This is distinct from the active development repository: the active repository may be deleted or repurposed after the service is sunset, but the archived snapshot provides a permanent reference for anyone who needs to understand how the service worked. The archive captures the final production state — not the most recent commit on the main branch, which may include undeployed changes.

The includes_deployment_configs field is important: infrastructure-as-code templates, Kubernetes manifests, and environment variable definitions are often stored separately from the application code. An archive that includes only the application source but not the deployment configuration is incomplete — someone reading the archive cannot understand how the service was deployed without the infrastructure code. The archive should capture both, and includes_deployment_configs in the SourceArchiveRecord provides a machine-checkable assertion that this was done.
Authoring the Decision Log

The decision log captures the architectural and design decisions made throughout the service's lifetime. It answers the question that new engineers always ask when they first encounter an unfamiliar system: "why is it done this way?" Without a decision log, the answer is always "we don't know, it's just always been like that" — which means the next service builder either repeats the same experiment (relearning why a rejected approach was rejected) or keeps the same decision without understanding its basis (unable to revisit it when the context changes).

The revisit_condition field is the most actionable part of an ADR. It states explicitly when the decision should be re-examined — "if Anthropic releases a model with native JSON mode that eliminates parsing errors" or "if request volume exceeds 10,000 per minute and latency becomes a constraint." A decision log without revisit conditions is a historical record; a decision log with them is a living guide that tells future engineers when to question inherited patterns. For the archival context, revisit conditions also help the replacement service team identify which design decisions are tied to conditions that no longer apply.
Incident History as Institutional Knowledge

Incident post-mortems are the most valuable form of operational documentation a service produces. They describe real failure modes in concrete detail: what the symptoms were, what the system state looked like from monitoring, what the team tried during the incident, and what the root cause turned out to be. For Claude integrations, incidents often reveal non-obvious model behaviour — how the model responds to unusual input patterns, what happens when token limits are exceeded mid-conversation, how safety filters interact with specific domain vocabulary. This knowledge is uniquely hard to reconstruct without the incident as a reference point.

The claude_behaviour_relevant flag enables a targeted extract: the replacement service team can filter the incident archive to only the incidents where Claude's model behaviour was a contributing factor. These incidents are the most transferable learning — they describe real interaction patterns that caused production failures, which the replacement service should evaluate against its own prompt design and error handling before deployment. Incidents where the root cause was infrastructure (database outage, CDN failure, deploy error) are less relevant for the model interaction design of a successor service.
Archival Completeness Gate

Like the data retention process, the archival process has a completeness gate that must be passed before infrastructure teardown. The gate checks that every BEFORE_TEARDOWN artefact is marked as complete, that the prompt library export includes the evaluation suite, and that the decision log covers the major architectural choices. An incomplete archival record discovered after infrastructure teardown cannot be remediated.

The decision_log_adequate method uses a minimum entry count of 3, which is a floor — a service with 50 significant architectural decisions should have more than 3 ADRs. The minimum enforces that the decision log is not empty, not that it is comprehensive. Teams should set a service-specific minimum during the archival planning phase based on the service's actual complexity. A service that was in production for 3 years and made 15 significant architectural decisions should archive all 15; a service that ran for 6 months with a straightforward architecture may legitimately have only 3-5 decisions worth recording.
Summary

Archival and knowledge transfer converts a sunset service's accumulated operational knowledge from tacit expertise into documented artefacts and explicit transfer sessions. The six archival categories — system design, prompt library, operational runbooks, decision log, incident history, and source snapshot — represent the outputs that have lasting value for future development. The prompt library and its evaluation suite are the highest-priority artefacts for Claude integrations: they encode months of iteration on model interaction quality and provide the baseline for any successor service. Structured knowledge transfer sessions complement documentation by capturing reasoning, rejected alternatives, and operational experience that prose does not easily convey. An archive that is completed before infrastructure teardown and accessible to successor teams is the final product of a service well-decommissioned.
